summary: This brief post teases Joel's Inc. magazine column about the perverse effects of commission-based sales incentives. Using the example of a shoe store cashier pushing useless silicone spray, Joel sets up an argument about how commission structures lead to bad customer experiences. His father adds a parallel to education policy, noting that measurable incentives like No Child Left Behind create similar distortions.
key_points: Commission-based incentives drive employees to push products that don't serve customers
Up-selling bogus products erodes customer trust and experience
The perverse incentive problem extends beyond sales to areas like education policy (No Child Left Behind)
money_quotes: It's supposed to make sneakers shiny and waterproof, but it doesn't seem to do anything.
The same problem arises when you set measurable incentives (money for better test results) in educational policies like No Child Left Behind.
key_insight: Commission and incentive structures often produce perverse outcomes, pushing people to optimize for the metric rather than the actual goal.
